content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 21,
1993
Jeanne C.
Byrne
City of Pacific Grove
300 Forest Avenue
Pacific Grove,
CA
93950
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93—153
Dear Ms.
Byrne:
You have requested advice regarding your duties as Mayor of
the City of Pacific Grove under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Please note that our advice is prospective in nature.
We do
not comment on any past conduct.
QUESTION
Under the Act,
may you represent a client before the planning
commission?
CONCLUSION
The Act prohibits you from attempting .to use your official
position to influence a decision before the planning commission.
FACTS
As Mayor of the City of Pacific Grove,
you sit as a member of
a seven person city council.
One of the council’s duties is to
appoint non—paid members to city boards.and commissions,
including
an architectural review board and planning commission.
While you
are solely responsible for nominating such appointees, the full
council must vote to appoint.
Matters decided by the
architectural review board and planning commission are appealable
to the city council.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—153
Page
2
You are a licensed architect and sole practitioner.
You and
your husband,
who is a general contractor,
own the firm of Byrne
and Byrne
& Associates, Inc.
Since many of your clients own
property in Pacific Grove, your practice has included submission
of plans to the city’s community development department,
discussion and negotiation with city staff and appearances before
the architectural review board, planning commission and city
council.
V
You would like to represent a client before the planning
V
commission this month.
The client is seeking a yard set back
variance and a parking variance.
You would like to present the
project to the planning commission and explain the reasons for the
variance requests.
ANALYSIS
V
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on
the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
*****
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans bya commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dol
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within
12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a),
(c)
and (d).


File No.
A—93—153
Page
3
Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making,
or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on your business or on any of your clients who
have been a source of income of more than $250 within 12 months of
a decision.
2
Prohibited “attempts to influence a decision” are defined in
Regulation 18700.1
(below)
and include contacting any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the official’s agency or any
agency under the appointive or budgetary control of the official’s
agency:
(a)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or sublect to the budgetary
control of his or her agency,
3 the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if,
for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.
Regulation 18700.1(a).
(Emphasis added.)
Pursuant to subdivision (a),
you may not appear before your
own agency,
which is the city council,
or any agency appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of the city council.
This
includes the architectural review board and the planning
commission, which are appointed by the city council.
You also may
2
Disqualification means that the official cannot participate
in discussions, vote on a matter,
or otherwise try to influence
members of the official’s own agency or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official’s agency.
Section 82041 defines “local government agency” as a county,
city, or district of any kind including “any department, division,
bureau, office,
board,
commission or other agency of the
foregoing.”
Generally, the Commission has treated different city
departments as different agencies.
For example,
a planning
commission is a distinct agency from the city council or the
department of city planning.
(Stout Advice Letter,
No.
1-88-313.)


File No.
A—93--153
Page
4
not contact any member or staff of any of these agencies to
attempt to influence a decision.
Therefore,
appearing before the planning commission to
represent a client would, under the law, constitute an-improper
use of your official position to influence a decision.
Regulation
18700.1 sets forth a limited exception wherein the official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if the official:
(4)
Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before any agency.
However, this provision applies
only if the official has no other direct oral or
written contact with the agency with regard to the
client’s proceeding before the agency except for
necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official.
Regulation 18700.1(b) (4).
(Emphasis added.)
The Act does not prohibit an employee of your architectural
business from representing an applicant before the planning
commission or the city council.
However,
you may not participate
in a city council decision if the decision will foreseeably and
materially affect your architectural business,
or a source of
income of $250 or more,
or any other economic interest of yours.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
J(i\Il
S echer
Coünse
,
Legal Division
JM:JS:ak

Under the Act,
may you represent a client before the planning
commission?

The Act prohibits you from attempting .to use your official
position to influence a decision before the planning commission.

QUESTION: Under the Act,
may you represent a client before the planning
commission?

CONCLUSION: The Act prohibits you from attempting .to use your official
position to influence a decision before the planning commission.
